text: The surgeon applies two Allis forceps on either side of the umbilicus to avert the crease. A stab wound is made at the inferior crease of the umbilicus using an #11 blade. The surgeon introduces a Veress needle and performs irrigation, suction, and hanging drop tests to confirm entry into the abdominal cavity, indicated by the appearance of bubbles.

A few drops of saline are placed on the abdominal wall, which is then lifted, allowing the saline to be sucked in. The surgeon starts the insufflator, using a Striker pneumoflator at a flow rate of one liter per minute. As gas enters the abdominal cavity, the liver dendrosis becomes obliterated, indicating successful insufflation.

The surgeon introduces the trocar into the abdominal cavity using a screwing motion, supported by the abdominal wall. Inside the abdomen, a large endometrioma is visible on the right ovary, surrounded by some fluid. The presence of endometriosis is noted, along with visible adhesions and sub-serous fibroids. The outer layer of the ovarian cortex is being separated using the harmonic device.

The surgeon uses an atraumatic grasper in the left hand and the vibrating jaw of the harmonic scalpel in the right hand to split the outer layer of the ovary. Despite efforts to prevent rupture, the endometrioma ruptures, and its contents visibly leak out.

The surgeon uses suction to remove the chocolate-colored material from the ruptured endometrioma cyst, preventing spillage. The cyst collapses, facilitating the ovarian cystectomy. The surgeon employs a grasper in the left hand and a Maryland dissector in the right hand to strip the thick cystic wall from the ovarian cortex, alternating hands to achieve a clean dissection plane.

The surgeon continues to strip the cystic wall from the ovary using ambidextrous techniques, ensuring no part of the cyst is left behind. The remaining chocolate material is slowly separated and suctioned out, with care taken to prevent leakage into the upper abdomen. The suction device helps identify a good plane for complete removal of the cyst contents.

The surgeon uses a Maryland and semi-traumatic grasper to continue the ovarian cystectomy, carefully separating the ovarian cyst from the ovary. The ovary is gently handled to preserve the cortex material, and approximately ninety percent of the cystic wall has been removed. The remaining cystic wall is separated with minimal effort, despite its tendency to slip.

The surgeon places the excised ovarian cyst over the anterior cul-de-sac to prevent it from being missed. The entire cystic wall is stripped out, completing the cystectomy procedure. A thorough lavage is performed to remove any leaked chocolate material. A small sub-serous myoma, approximately 15 millimeters in size, is excised, followed by the removal of another myoma measuring approximately 30 millimeters.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to perform a simple myoma excision, staying close to the fibroid to avoid damaging surrounding serous layers. The myoma is successfully separated without the need for suturing, as minimal energy application prevents bleeding.

The surgeon uses a stone holding forceps to extract myomas, starting with the first one measuring approximately fifteen millimeters. The second myoma is held longitudinally to avoid transverse arrest and is pulled out without the need for morcellation. An endo bag with a metal ring is then used to collect the cystic content, utilizing a grasper to drop the material into the double-layer polypropylene bag, which is designed to be strong and tear-resistant.

The surgeon places the cyst inside a commercially available endo bag with a metal ring, which closes with a purse string effect. The endo bag is then pulled out, acting as a wound protector to prevent contamination. An ovum forceps is used to extract the cyst from the endo bag. The uterus is lifted, and an interseed, a deoxygenated cellulose material, is applied to prevent adhesion between the uterus and rectum after adhesolysis.

The surgeon spreads the interseed material along the posterior wall of the uterus, covering the wound from the posterior myoma and preventing adhesion with the omentum or rectum. The injured serous layer of the uterus is also covered with the interseed.

The surgeon carefully opens the serous layer adjacent to the cornea to remove a small corneal fibroid measuring approximately one centimeter. The fibroid is located near the tube, and its removal is performed to prevent potential tubal blockage as it grows. The fibroid is excised without leaving any remnants inside, ensuring the patient's tubes remain unobstructed for future fertility.

The surgeon carefully removes the myoma without damaging the adjacent tube, ensuring the entire myoma is extracted through a ten millimeter port. Thorough suction irrigation is performed, followed by port closure using a suture and facial closure needle to prevent future hernia formation. The rectus muscle and peritoneum are secured in one knot, and the port is tightly closed. A final examination confirms the surgical site is clear before the telescope is withdrawn.
